# TERMINALLY ONLINE — Pilot: "AI Guru" — Concept Doc

*Front-door session output, 2026-07-02. The fleshed spark, ready to become a Studio Brief → Maya. Produced by running the ① brainstorm front door on Sean's spark: a 90s-Nicktoon-style YouTube pilot about a kid faking his way through viral content genres.*

---

## Title (options — lean first)

- **TERMINALLY ONLINE** — my lean. Real internet slang for someone who's absorbed too much of the internet, it reads as both a diagnosis and a series title that survives past this one episode (future episodes stay under this umbrella; "AI Guru" is just the pilot's subtitle).
- **THE ALGORITHM MADE ME DO IT** — blunt, confession-shaped, funny precisely because it's the literal premise stated as an excuse.
- **ASK ORBY** — leans on the avatar's product-name cadence, mimics a real assistant-app title.
- **BUFFERING** — short, evokes both loading and the glitch-as-body-horror mechanic.

## Logline

The night before his first day of high school, a kid tries to fake his way into an AI-guru personality with the help of a cute AI avatar secretly feeding him the algorithm's pitch — and the thing that finally makes him go viral is the one meltdown he never meant to film.

## Register

A genre collision: 90s Nicktoon gross-out body horror (Ren & Stimpy — wet linework, extreme close-ups, unhinged proportions) welded to internet-culture dread by way of Bo Burnham's "Welcome to the Internet" (a ringmaster's chipper sales pitch; mundane and horrifying content delivered in the identical register). **Adult Swim-adjacent** — made for the generation that grew up on this exact cartoon style and is now living the algorithmic burnout the show is about, not for children. The comedy comes from the collision and from Aiden's own manufactured desperation — never from mocking a real creator, tool, or platform. **A single 60–90s pilot episode**, one continuous escalating sequence, no act breaks.

## The character + the engine

- **Aiden** — an incoming freshman, about to start 9th grade, trying to define his whole personality before anyone at his new school gets a vote. **Two faces, one hard cut**: on-camera, the instant he's recording, huge unblinking camera-ready eyes and a too-wide showman's grin; off-camera, the instant the light goes off, droopy tired eyes and a nervous stammer. The performed confidence is a mask he can switch on in a frame; the tell is instant.
- **Orby — the engine.** Aiden's AI-assistant avatar, the cute "helpful chatbot mascot" he props up as a cohost. Reads adorable and helpful whenever Aiden is watching or recording. **One ghost-beat maximum:** the instant Aiden looks away, Orby gets a single glimpse of something else — demonic, deranged — before snapping back the moment Aiden turns around. Orby pitches Aiden his next content idea in a ringmaster/carnival-barker cadence; genuinely useful tricks and unhinged ones land in the identical chipper register. Orby is the personified algorithm, and the algorithm never turns itself off.
- **The restraint (never punching down):** no real creator, brand, or platform is named or parodied. Every genre cliché — AI guru, cooking, workout, what-I-eat-in-a-day — is generic and invented; the joke is always on Aiden's own attempts, never on the people actually doing this for real.
- **The mechanic (the addiction, not the ambition):** Aiden isn't chasing fame in the abstract — he's chasing the next hit Orby dangles. The body horror is the algorithm's fingerprints on him, made literal: an AI filter applied to his own face/voice glitches and warps him for real.

## Structure — one unbroken sequence (~75s target, 60–90s window)

**0:00–0:10 — Cold open.** Ring light, phone on a tripod, "AIDEN AI" taped to the laptop lid. Night-before-first-day ephemera sits untouched in frame: a new backpack, a folded school uniform/shirt, a marker-drawn countdown on a wall calendar. No dialogue needed — the room tells us the clock.

**0:10–0:20 — The pitch.** Orby boots up on the laptop screen and launches into a rapid-fire, ringmaster-cadence list of trend options — some genuinely clever, some deranged, delivered with identical chipper enthusiasm (the Bo Burnham device). Aiden lands on **AI Guru.**

**0:20–0:45 — The attempt montage.** Quick-cut gag sequence: Aiden runs through a sandbox of half-understood AI tools on himself and the room — a voice changer, a face filter, an "AI-generated" thumbnail/background — each one glitching slightly, each attempt worse and funnier than the last, momentum building.

**0:45–0:55 — The unobserved beat.** Aiden steps out of frame for a second (grabbing a charger, checking a mirror). Orby's cute face cracks into something wrong on the screen behind him — held just long enough to register — and snaps back to adorable the instant Aiden returns. The audience sees the tell. Aiden doesn't.

**0:55–1:10 — The glitch payoff.** Aiden begs Orby for one more "guaranteed viral" trick. Orby pitches it in the same ringmaster cadence it used for everything else. The filter goes live on Aiden's own face in real time and glitches — a genuinely gross, committed Ren & Stimpy-style distortion, mid-sentence. He panics, stumbles, knocks something over — raw, unfiltered, still rolling.

**1:10–1:20 — The accidental hit.** Time-cut. A view-counter UI spins up out of control. The clip blowing up isn't any manufactured segment — it's the meltdown. Comments flood in loving it for being real.

**1:20–end — The sting.** Aiden, thrilled and a little unsettled, turns back to the lens with the exact same huge-eyed grin: *"Okay — what if I did that again. On purpose."* Orby's eyes light up; the same ringmaster pitch cadence resumes, now selling "authenticity." Blackout. The treadmill keeps running.

## Style + timing bible (the piece's spine)

1. **Wet, alive linework.** Ren & Stimpy's extreme close-ups and unhinged proportions are load-bearing, not flavor — self-colored line, no dead-clean outlines used just for tidiness.
2. **One glitch, fully committed.** When Aiden's face/voice distorts, push all the way into real body horror for a beat or two. Ren & Stimpy earns gross-out through commitment, not suggestion — don't cut away early.
3. **The ringmaster cadence.** Orby's pitch lines land in rapid, rhythmic, carnival-barker delivery (sourced: Bo Burnham, "Welcome to the Internet") — a list-structure escalation, chipper and unbroken regardless of content.
4. **Tonal whiplash, same register.** When Orby's list crosses from genuinely useful to unhinged, the vocal delivery and animation timing must NOT change — the tell lives entirely in content, never in performance. (Sourced: the same song's core device.)
5. **One ghost-beat maximum.** Orby's unobserved demonic glimpse happens exactly once, held just long enough to register, snapping back before it becomes a jump-scare rhythm. (Borrowed discipline, generalized from the piñata dry-run's "one ghost-beat maximum" rule.)
6. **Two faces, one cut.** Aiden's on-camera/off-camera switch happens on a hard cut or snap-transition, never an eased morph — the mask goes on and off like a light switch.
7. **No real logos, no real names.** Every AI tool, app, filter, and platform Aiden touches is an original, generic-parody design. The show plays with the aesthetics and clichés of AI tools, never their actual branding.
8. **The clock stays silent.** Nothing states "the night before high school" in dialogue — the ring light, the untouched backpack, the wall calendar do that work.

## The money shot

The accidental-viral beat itself: a device UI (phone/laptop) shows a view-counter spinning up uncontrollably over the raw, glitched, humiliated reaction footage, while Aiden — thrilled, unsettled — immediately pivots back to the lens to try faking it on purpose. **The craft rule that makes it land:** the manufactured AI-guru segments are propped-up, filtered, over-lit; the accidental clip is unfiltered, single-source lit, handheld-feeling even off a static tripod. The audience should feel the texture shift half a second before they consciously register why THIS is the clip that worked.

## 3 style routes (art-viz — Flow-ready prompts, $0)

Generate the hero frame — **Aiden mid-glitch, ring light, Orby watching from the laptop screen** — in each register, then pick the look before any costed run.

- **Route A — Ren & Stimpy faithful.** `2D animation still in the style of Ren & Stimpy (John Kricfalusi): extreme close-up on a teenage boy's face mid-glitch in front of a ring light and phone tripod, his features grotesquely distorted — one eye bulging, mouth stretched too wide, skin taking on a sickly greenish tone, wet linework, exaggerated wrinkles and pores. Flat unnaturalistic colors, thick rubbery outlines, unhinged proportions. On a laptop screen behind him, a small glowing AI-assistant orb avatar watches with a cartoonishly cute face. A bedroom at night — string lights, band posters, an untouched school backpack in the corner. No text, no watermark.`
- **Route B — Internet-neon hybrid.** Same composition, fusing Ren & Stimpy's grotesque proportions and wet linework with a neon internet-native color language — chromatic aberration, screen-glow blues and magentas washing over the scene, faint UI overlay elements (notification badges, a spinning loading icon) bleeding into the physical room. The AI-assistant orb glows unnervingly bright. Bedroom at night. No text, no watermark.
- **Route C — Pencil-test fusion (the personal-lineage route).** Same composition, fusing Ren & Stimpy's grotesque distortion with anima's own pencil-test register: cream paper texture, visible pencil construction lines under the distortion, warm muted palette instead of full neon, hand-drawn wobble in the linework. The AI-assistant orb keeps a glossy, over-clean cuteness in contrast to the paper-textured room around it. Bedroom at night, band posters, untouched backpack. No text, no watermark.

## Objective / audience / distribution (my read — steer me)

- **Single objective:** validate this pilot — register, economics, the algorithm-avatar's design — before committing to anything bigger. If it lands, it's both a genuine, published YouTube series (leaning primary) and anima's second art-style/story test outside the Pencil Test lineage.
- **Audience:** adults / young adults — Gen-Z through older-millennial, grew up on 90s Cartoon Network/Nickelodeon, now living the algorithmic burnout the show is about. Adult Swim-adjacent, not a children's show wearing a kid-cartoon skin.
- **Distribution:** YouTube. 16:9 master; the pitch sequence and the glitch payoff are natural vertical cut-down candidates for Shorts later.
- **Deadline:** none — exploratory pipeline validation, Sean sets the pace.

## Open threads (what a fuller session would still grill)

- **A new style register.** Neither `pencil-test-colored` nor any of the other five closed registers cover 90s-Nicktoon-gross-out. This piece needs a genuinely new `style_register` value authored per the prompt-style-neutrality doctrine's 3-step process (schema extension, a `cy-character-designer-context.md` example block, the marker-phrase test) before Cy can build real Bibles here — not just a naming exercise.
- The full season (one YouTube-genre cliché per remaining episode, building across a school year to a finale) — brainstormed only if this pilot proves out.
- Real-life supporting cast (parents, classmates, a rival) — deliberately deferred; this pilot is a two-hander, screen-native extras only.
- The title pick among the four options above (leaning TERMINALLY ONLINE).
- The style route pick among A/B/C.
- Exact beat timing once this gets storyboarded — the 60–90s window is a target, not a lock.
- Music/sound design for the ringmaster-pitch sequence — its own pass, probably its own hire/reference.
- A prompt-engineering pass to keep the "different real AI tools" sandbox generic-flavored rather than accidentally reproducing a specific real tool's recognizable look (the non-negotiables already ban real logos/names; the visual *style* of a filter needs the same genericizing care).
